findings.title: createAiOrchestrator is orphan code with no entry point wiring
findings.code_snippet: export function createAiOrchestrator(config: AiOrchestratorConfig): AiOrchestrator {
findings.description: createAiOrchestrator is exported but not imported by any entry point: no setup file (compare with createAnimaOrchestrator which has setup-orchestrator.ts), no DI registration via injeca, no IPC handler, no event listener. The only consumer is the test file. Grepping the entire apps/stage-tamagotchi/src tree and the full monorepo confirms zero non-test imports. This is dead-on-arrival code per the orphan detection rule.
findings.suggestion: Create a setup-ai-orchestrator.ts (similar to setup-orchestrator.ts) that wires createAiOrchestrator into the Electron main process, registers it with injeca DI, and exposes it via IPC to the renderer. Without entry point wiring, this code cannot execute in production.

findings.title: No logging in ai-orchestrator module (@guiiai/logg not used)
findings.code_snippet:
import { McpHub } from '@proj-airi/mcp-hub'
import { buildSkillsContext, SkillRegistry } from '@proj-airi/skills-engine'
import { generateText, stepCountIs } from 'ai'
findings.description: The ai-orchestrator module has zero logging. @guiiai/logg is not imported. The sibling orchestrator.ts in the same directory uses useLogg for structured logging. Key operations that need logging: initialize() (MCP connection results, skills loaded count), generate() (LLM call start/finish, token usage, duration), shutdown() (cleanup status). Without logging, production debugging of MCP connection failures, LLM errors, or performance issues will be impossible.
findings.suggestion: Add `import { useLogg } from '@guiiai/logg'` and create a logger: `const log = useLogg('ai-orchestrator').useGlobalConfig()`. Log at INFO level in initialize() (connected/failed counts), generate() (model ID, message count, token usage, duration_ms), and shutdown(). Log at ERROR level for failures.

findings.title: generate() has no error handling around generateText LLM call
findings.code_snippet:
const result = await generateText({
  model,
  system: systemPrompt,
  messages: messages as Array<...>,
  tools: tools as Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools'],
  stopWhen: stepCountIs(options.maxSteps ?? 5),
})
findings.description: The generateText() call performs an external LLM API request (network I/O) with zero error handling. This is the most critical I/O operation in the orchestrator. Network timeouts, rate limits, invalid API keys, model unavailability, and malformed responses will all produce raw, untyped exceptions that propagate to the caller with no context about what operation failed or what inputs were used. The caller receives a raw AI SDK error with no indication it came from the orchestrator's generate() method.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch. Catch operational errors (network, rate-limit, auth) and re-throw as a typed OrchestratorError with context: `try { ... } catch (err) { throw new Error('AI orchestrator generate failed', { cause: err }) }`. Include model ID, message count, and whether tools were provided in the error context. Consider distinguishing retryable (network timeout, rate limit) from non-retryable (auth, invalid model) errors.

findings.title: initialize() uses Promise.all for independent I/O -- partial failure unhandled
findings.code_snippet:
const [mcpResult] = await Promise.all([
  mcpHub.connectEnabled(),
  skillRegistry.loadAll(),
])
findings.description: initialize() runs mcpHub.connectEnabled() and skillRegistry.loadAll() via Promise.all(). These are independent I/O operations (network + filesystem). If skillRegistry.loadAll() throws (e.g., builtinSkillsDir does not exist or has unreadable files), Promise.all rejects immediately while the mcpHub.connectEnabled() promise may still be running. The MCP connection result is lost, and worse, the MCP connections may complete in the background without the caller knowing they succeeded -- creating zombie connections with no reference for cleanup. Additionally, there is no try/catch around the Promise.all, so raw filesystem errors from skills loading propagate with no orchestrator context.
findings.suggestion: Replace Promise.all with Promise.allSettled to handle each result independently. This ensures both operations complete regardless of individual failures. Then inspect each result: if skills failed, include the error in InitResult (add a skillsError field) rather than throwing. If MCP failed entirely (not per-server, which connectEnabled already handles), also surface it. Wrap in try/catch for unexpected errors. Example: `const [mcpSettled, skillsSettled] = await Promise.allSettled([...])` then check each `.status`.

findings.title: shutdown() does not protect against errors -- resources may leak
findings.description: shutdown() calls mcpHub.shutdown() without try/catch. While mcpHub.shutdown() internally uses try/finally (disconnectAll + store.close), if mcpHub.shutdown() itself throws (e.g., the underlying store.close() throws a synchronous error after disconnectAll), the error propagates raw. More importantly, the orchestrator only cleans up MCP resources. The SkillRegistry is not cleaned up (though it currently has no shutdown method, this is a design gap). If future cleanup steps are added after mcpHub.shutdown(), an error from MCP shutdown would prevent them from running. The pattern should use try/finally to ensure all cleanup runs.
findings.suggestion: Add try/catch with contextual error wrapping. Even though mcpHub.shutdown() has its own try/finally, the orchestrator should catch and wrap: `try { await mcpHub.shutdown() } catch (err) { throw new Error('AI orchestrator shutdown failed', { cause: err }) }`. If additional cleanup is added later, use try/finally to ensure all resources are released.

findings.title: No error path tests for AiOrchestrator.initialize() failures
findings.code_snippet:
it('creates orchestrator, initializes, builds prompt with skills, gets MCP tools, generates', async () => {
  ...
  expect(initResult.mcpConnected).toHaveLength(1)
  expect(initResult.mcpFailed).toHaveLength(0)
  ...
findings.description: The orchestrator's initialize() method handles MCP connection failures by returning them in mcpFailed, and the source code at ai-orchestrator.ts:87-89 maps failed connections to { id, error } objects. However, no test verifies this failure path. There is no test for: (1) an MCP server that fails to connect (returns in mcpFailed), (2) generateText throwing an error during generation, (3) aggregateTools failing when no servers are connected. The initialize() happy path is tested but the error paths are completely uncovered. Since this is the integration checkpoint that wires MCP + Skills + AI SDK together, failure modes are critical to validate.
findings.suggestion: Add integration tests that: (1) Register an MCP server with an invalid command (e.g., 'nonexistent-binary') and verify initialize() returns it in mcpFailed with a meaningful error string. (2) Create a test model whose doGenerate throws an error, call orchestrator.generate(), and verify the error propagates correctly. (3) Call getTools() before initialize() and verify graceful behavior.

findings.title: generate() has no error handling - raw errors propagate
findings.description: The generate() method calls generateText() (AI SDK) with no try/catch. If the LLM call fails (network timeout, rate limit, invalid model, malformed response), the error propagates without context about what operation failed, which model was used, or what the input was. The sibling orchestrator.ts wraps operations in try/catch with errorHandler that adds context. This is an Imperative Shell boundary that should handle errors gracefully.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the generateText call in try/catch. On failure, log the error with context (model ID, message count, system prompt length) and either re-throw a typed error with context or return a Result type.

findings.title: buildSystemPrompt() has hidden mutation - activates skills cumulatively
findings.description: buildSystemPrompt() mutates the SkillRegistry by calling activate() for each provided skill ID, but never deactivates previously activated skills. This means: (1) calling buildSystemPrompt(['a']) then buildSystemPrompt(['b']) results in both 'a' and 'b' being active, (2) calling buildSystemPrompt() without arguments uses whatever was previously activated as a side effect of prior calls, (3) the method name suggests a pure builder/getter but actually mutates shared state.
findings.suggestion: Either: (a) Deactivate all skills before activating the requested ones. Or (b) separate the concerns: make activate/deactivate explicit caller responsibilities and make buildSystemPrompt() purely read the current state without mutating it.

findings.title: No config validation - empty paths produce confusing downstream errors
findings.description: AiOrchestratorConfig fields (mcpDbPath, builtinSkillsDir, userSkillsDir) are not validated. An empty string for mcpDbPath will create an in-memory SQLite database (silent behavior change). Empty or non-existent paths for skills directories will silently load zero skills. These are external boundary inputs that should be validated early.
findings.suggestion: Add validation at the top of createAiOrchestrator: `if (!config.mcpDbPath) throw new Error('mcpDbPath is required'); if (!config.builtinSkillsDir) throw new Error('builtinSkillsDir is required');`. Consider using valibot (project standard) for schema validation of the config object.

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' type casts bypass type safety in generate()
findings.code_snippet:
messages: messages as Array<{ role: 'user' | 'assistant', content: string }>,
tools: tools as Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools'],
stopWhen: stepCountIs(options.maxSteps ?? 5),
findings.description: Two `as` type casts are used to force types into generateText(). The `tools` cast is particularly concerning: aggregateTools() returns Record<string, unknown> which is cast to the AI SDK's tool type. If the actual MCP tool format does not match the AI SDK's expected tool schema, this will cause runtime errors that TypeScript cannot catch.
findings.suggestion: For tools: define a proper return type from aggregateTools() that matches the AI SDK tool interface, or create a mapping function that validates/transforms the MCP tool format to AI SDK format with runtime checks. For messages: use the AI SDK's own message types from '@ai-sdk/provider' in the generate() signature.

findings.title: toolCalls/toolResults default to empty array -- hides missing data
findings.code_snippet:
toolCalls: (result.toolCalls ?? []).map(...),
toolResults: (result.toolResults ?? []).map(...),
findings.description: When result.toolCalls or result.toolResults are undefined/null (which happens if the AI SDK version changes its response shape or if the model returns an unexpected structure), the code silently defaults to empty arrays. The caller cannot distinguish between 'the model made no tool calls' and 'the response was malformed and tool call data was missing'.
findings.suggestion: This is acceptable defensive coding for AI SDK compatibility, but consider logging a warning when these fields are unexpectedly nullish (i.e., when the finishReason is 'tool-calls' but toolCalls is nullish).

findings.title: Comment claims structure not verified by assertions
findings.code_snippet:
// Tool should be a callable object with type and execute
expect(typeof tool).toBe('object')
findings.description: The comment states 'Tool should be a callable object with type and execute' implying the tool has 'type' and 'execute' properties. However, the only assertion is `typeof tool === 'object'`, which does not verify any specific properties. A future maintainer reading this comment would believe these properties are tested when they are not.
findings.suggestion: Either add assertions that verify the 'type' and 'execute' properties (e.g., `expect(tool).toHaveProperty('execute')`) or change the comment to accurately describe what is being tested: `// Tool should be a defined object`.

findings.title: AiOrchestrator exposes mcpHub and skillRegistry as public properties
findings.code_snippet:
readonly mcpHub: McpHub
readonly skillRegistry: SkillRegistry
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.8). The AiOrchestrator interface exposes mcpHub and skillRegistry as public readonly properties. While readonly prevents reassignment, it does not prevent calling mutating methods on these objects. The test file exploits this to add servers and activate skills directly, bypassing the orchestrator's own methods. The factory function creates both objects internally but then leaks them, undermining the encapsulation that factory+interface pattern is supposed to provide.
findings.suggestion: Remove mcpHub and skillRegistry from the AiOrchestrator interface. Instead, expose specific methods the consumer needs: addServer(config), activateSkill(id), getServerStatus(id). Keep mcpHub and skillRegistry as closure-scoped variables in createAiOrchestrator, similar to how AnimaOrchestrator in Task 7 hides emotionActor and pipeline.

findings.title: getTools() returns Record<string, unknown> -- propagates untyped tools
findings.code_snippet: getTools(): Promise<Record<string, unknown>>
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 4/10, Enforcement: N/A (Aggregate: 4.7). The getTools() method returns Record<string, unknown>, which provides no type information about what an AI SDK tool looks like. This forces unsafe casts downstream at line 129. Since AiOrchestrator is meant to be the integration surface, it has the opportunity to define a proper AiTool type or use AI SDK's Tool type alias.
findings.suggestion: Import the CoreTool type from the 'ai' package (or the appropriate AI SDK type for tools) and use it as the return type: `getTools(): Promise<Record<string, CoreTool>>`. Alternatively, define an `AiSdkTool` interface that captures the expected shape.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` casts in generate() bypass type safety
findings.code_snippet:
messages: messages as Array<{ role: 'user' | 'assistant', content: string }>,
tools: tools as Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools'],
findings.description: Encapsulation: N/A, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 4/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 3.3). Two unsafe casts in the generate() method. Both casts indicate a gap between the orchestrator's own type definitions and the AI SDK's expected types.
findings.suggestion: For messages: use AI SDK's CoreMessage type directly in the generate() signature instead of defining a custom narrower type. For tools: fix the getTools() return type so the cast becomes unnecessary. If the AI SDK types are not directly usable, create an explicit adapter function with proper type narrowing rather than using `as` casts.

findings.title: No boundary tests for buildSystemPrompt with edge inputs
findings.description: buildSystemPrompt() is tested only with valid skill IDs and a non-empty base prompt. Missing boundary conditions: (1) Calling with no arguments and no base prompt. (2) Passing nonexistent skill IDs. (3) Calling before initialize() (skills not loaded). These edge cases could produce unexpected empty prompts or throw errors.
findings.suggestion: Add boundary condition tests for empty prompt, nonexistent skill IDs, and pre-initialization calls.

findings.title: generate() not tested with empty messages or maxSteps edge values
findings.code_snippet:
const result = await orchestrator.generate(model, [
  { role: 'user', content: 'Hello, Anima!' },
])

expect(result.text).toBe('Hello from test model')
findings.description: generate() is tested with a single valid user message. Boundary conditions not covered: (1) Empty messages array. (2) maxSteps set to 0 or 1. (3) Very large maxSteps value. Since generate() wraps AI SDK's generateText with stepCountIs stop condition, boundary behavior of the step count parameter matters for preventing runaway agent loops.
findings.suggestion: Add boundary tests for generate() with empty messages, maxSteps=1, and maxSteps=0.

findings.title: Test function exceeds 50 lines with inline model duplication
findings.description: This test function spans 71 lines. A significant chunk (lines 367-392) defines an inline LanguageModelV2 model that duplicates the doStream implementation from createTestModel (lines 54-63).
findings.suggestion: Extend createTestModel to accept a doGenerate override, eliminating ~10 lines of duplicated doStream boilerplate and shortening the test to under 50 lines.

findings.title: doStream implementation duplicated in test file
findings.description: The doStream block at lines 382-391 is a near-verbatim copy of lines 54-63 in createTestModel. Only the textDelta string and usage numbers differ. This is exactly the type of duplication that the createTestModel factory was built to eliminate.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate by reusing createTestModel with a doGenerate override. The doStream is never actually called in these tests, so a single implementation suffices.

findings.title: Comment references transient SDK internals (v2 compat mode)
findings.code_snippet: // Verify tool calls across steps (v2 compat mode stores in steps)
findings.description: The phrase 'v2 compat mode stores in steps' references a specific AI SDK implementation detail. As the AI SDK evolves, this comment will become stale and confusing. The 'compat mode' framing suggests this is a temporary workaround, functioning as an implicit TODO.
findings.suggestion: Simplify to describe what is being tested without referencing internal SDK versioning: `// Verify tool calls are recorded in steps`.

findings.title: Comment references internal dependency @ai-sdk/mcp
findings.code_snippet: // AI SDK tools from @ai-sdk/mcp have specific structure
findings.description: The comment references '@ai-sdk/mcp' which is an internal dependency of '@proj-airi/mcp-hub', not directly imported in this test. A future maintainer may search for '@ai-sdk/mcp' in the test's direct dependencies and be confused when they don't find it.
findings.suggestion: Reference the direct API instead: `// McpHub aggregated tools have a specific AI SDK-compatible structure`.

findings.title: Magic number 5 as default maxSteps should be a named constant
findings.code_snippet: stopWhen: stepCountIs(options.maxSteps ?? 5),
findings.description: The default value 5 for maxSteps is a magic number. Its meaning ('default maximum number of agent loop steps') is not self-documenting.
findings.suggestion: Extract to a named constant: `const DEFAULT_MAX_STEPS = 5` at the top of the file or inside createAiOrchestrator.

findings.title: Double type assertion on messages and tools in generate()
findings.code_snippet:
messages: messages as Array<{ role: 'user' | 'assistant', content: string }>,
tools: tools as Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools'],
findings.description: The messages parameter is cast to its own declared type (redundant). The tools cast uses a complex Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools'] extraction. If getTools() returned the correct type directly, both casts could be eliminated.
findings.suggestion: Define a type alias `type AiSdkTools = Parameters<typeof generateText>[0]['tools']`, update getTools() return type, and eliminate both inline casts.

findings.title: GenerateResult.usage inner object fields lack readonly modifier
findings.code_snippet: readonly usage: { inputTokens: number | undefined, outputTokens: number | undefined }
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 7/10 (Aggregate: 7.0). The GenerateResult interface correctly uses readonly on top-level fields, but the nested usage object's properties are not readonly. `result.usage.inputTokens = 999` would be allowed by the type checker.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to the nested usage fields: `readonly usage: { readonly inputTokens: number | undefined, readonly outputTokens: number | undefined }`.

positive_observations: Factory function pattern (createAiOrchestrator) follows the established Functional Core / Imperative Shell convention used by the sibling createAnimaOrchestrator
Test file uses real MCP server fixture (echo-mcp-server.ts) and real SkillRegistry with real file system - excellent integration test pattern
Test Double for LanguageModelV2 has proper rationale comment explaining why external LLM API cannot be used in tests
Clean interface-first design: AiOrchestrator interface defined separately from implementation, enabling future alternative implementations
End-to-end integration test exercises the full createAiOrchestrator -> initialize -> buildSystemPrompt -> getTools -> generate path
Test verifies actual MCP tool names (echo, add) and skill content (warm, caring AI companion) - not just shape assertions
Test Double rationale comments on both createTestModel and the inline model follow CLAUDE.md conventions perfectly
JSDoc on AiOrchestratorConfig and AiOrchestrator interface is comprehensive and accurate
No forbidden patterns (TODO, FIXME, HACK, XXX, PLACEHOLDER, TEMP) found in any changed file
Real MCP server (echo-mcp-server.ts via stdio transport) is used for integration tests instead of mocking MCP connections - proper dev/prod parity
Real SQLite databases created in temp directories for McpHub - no InMemoryRepository or mock DB
Real SkillRegistry with real filesystem skills directory - tests prove actual SKILL.md loading works
Test structure follows progressive integration: individual components tested first, then full orchestrator end-to-end
Proper cleanup with afterEach removing temp directories and shutting down MCP hubs
Good use of InitResult to surface per-server MCP connection failures (mcpFailed array) rather than throwing on first failure
Error conversion in initialize(): String(f.error) safely converts unknown error types to strings
Individual methods inside the orchestrator are short and focused (initialize: 15 lines, buildSystemPrompt: 21 lines, shutdown: 3 lines)
Guard clause pattern used in buildSystemPrompt keeps nesting shallow at depth 2
AiOrchestratorConfig uses readonly modifiers on all fields - good immutable config pattern
InitResult and GenerateResult are well-structured readonly result types that avoid leaking implementation details
